doctors.bio: I am an Ivy League-educated cardiologist with over 12 years of clinical experience specializing in non-invasive cardiology and preventive health.

doctors.name: Dr. Marcus Vance
doctors.bio: Dr. Marcus Vance is a board-certified pediatrician dedicated to providing comprehensive healthcare for children from infancy through adolescence.

doctors.name: Dr. Aaliyah Patel
doctors.bio: Dr. Aaliyah Patel is a passionate family physician specializing in chronic disease management and virtual healthcare delivery paradigms.

consultations.reason: The sensation of a racing, fluttering, or skipping heartbeat.
consultations.notes:
History of Present Illness

36-year-old male reports intermittent palpitations described as “fluttering” and “skipping beats,” occurring several times daily over the past 72 hours. Episodes last 1–5 minutes and are associated with mild anxiety and lightheadedness. Denies chest pain, syncope, severe shortness of breath, fever, or recent illness.

Reports increased caffeine intake (4–5 energy drinks/day) and poor sleep over the past week. No known cardiac history.

Review of Systems
Cardiovascular: Positive for palpitations
Respiratory: Negative for dyspnea at rest
Neurologic: Negative for syncope
Constitutional: Negative for fever/chills
Medical History
Mild generalized anxiety disorder
No hypertension
No diabetes
Medications
None currently
Allergies
NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies)
Family History
Father with hypertension
No family history of sudden cardiac death
Social History
Non-smoker
Social alcohol use
High caffeine consumption
Objective (O)
Vital Signs
BP: 128/82 mmHg
HR: 104 bpm
RR: 18/min
Temp: 98.4°F
SpO₂: 99%
Physical Examination
General: Alert, mildly anxious, no acute distress
Cardiovascular: Mild tachycardia, regular rhythm during exam
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally
Neuro: Grossly intact
Diagnostic Data
In-office ECG: Sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemic changes
Recommended labs:
CBC
CMP
TSH
Magnesium
Assessment (A)
Diagnoses
Palpitations
Sinus tachycardia likely exacerbated by caffeine intake and anxiety
Anxiety disorder
Plan (P)
Advise reduction of caffeine and stimulant intake
Encourage hydration and sleep hygiene
Order outpatient Holter monitor if symptoms persist
ER precautions reviewed:
Chest pain
Fainting
Severe shortness of breath
Sustained rapid heartbeat
Follow-Up
Re-evaluation in 1–2 weeks
Earlier follow-up if symptoms worsen
consultations.prescription: Medication 1  Medication: Propranolol 10 mg tablet  Sig: Take 1 tablet by mouth twice daily as needed for palpitations and anxiety symptoms.  Dispense: 30 tablets Refills: 0  Indication: Symptomatic palpitations  Special Instructions: Avoid excessive caffeine intake. Monitor for dizziness, fatigue, or low heart rate.  Medication 2 (Optional Supportive Therapy)  Medication: Magnesium Oxide 400 mg tablet  Sig: Take 1 tablet by mouth once daily with food.  Dispense: 30 tablets Refills: 1  Limit caffeine, nicotine, and energy drinks Maintain adequate hydration Seek emergency care if: Chest pain develops You faint Heart rate remains very fast for prolonged periods Severe breathing difficulty occurs

consultations.reason: shortness of breath, palpitations and dizziness
Having trouble catching MY breath, even with minimal exertion or while resting.

consultations.notes: Referred to Dr. Marcus Vance (Pediatric Medicine Specialist).
